does: This paper systematically evaluates what large language models can contribute to reinforcement learning and sequential decision-making across diverse interactive domains. The authors test two approaches: directly generating actions as policies, and indirectly generating reward functions that guide RL training. Their central finding is that LLMs are particularly effective at reward modeling without task-specific fine-tuning, especially when using AI feedback to specify rewards. They demonstrate that AI-generated rewards improve credit assignment and exploration, and further show that fine-tuning LLMs on synthetic data substantially enhances reward modeling while avoiding catastrophic forgetting in unfamiliar environments. The work reveals LLMs as versatile components in RL systems rather than just action generators.
connects: This work builds on the emerging intersection of large language models and reinforcement learning, extending prior research exploring whether LLMs can serve as reasoners and planners. It responds to recent successes in using pretrained models for downstream tasks and builds on reward modeling approaches from inverse RL and RLHF literature. The paper connects to research on AI feedback and preference-based learning, which have shown promise in improving RL agent training without hand-crafted rewards. It also engages with literature on transfer learning and fine-tuning strategies in large models, particularly work addressing catastrophic forgetting when adapting pretrained models to new domains with synthetic data.
enables: This work opens pathways for leveraging LLMs as modular components in RL systems where reward specification is a bottleneck. It suggests future research on scaling reward modeling to complex multi-objective environments and investigating how LLM-generated rewards transfer across different task families. The findings enable development of more sample-efficient RL agents by outsourcing reward design to pretrained language models, and point toward hybrid systems that combine LLM reasoning with classical RL algorithms. Additionally, the approach of using synthetic data for fine-tuning without forgetting suggests possibilities for continually adapting LLMs to new domains, and raises questions about when and how to integrate language model priors into sequential decision-making pipelines in safety-critical settings.
